name: Brahmadatta
dates_notes: Cited by Sureśvara and Vāchaspati; no surviving work; positions reconstructed from later citations.
dating_methodology: Brahmadatta is dated by citation-evidence alone. Terminus ante quem: Sureśvara's *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* (later eighth century, by Sureśvara's standard placement as Śaṅkara's direct disciple in the post-720 CE generation) names him as a doctrinal opponent on whom the immediacy-of-knowledge thesis must be defended. Maṇḍana Miśra's *Brahma-Siddhi* (early eighth century, on the Hiriyanna-Thrasher dating) preserves a related *prasaṃkhyāna* position whose later commentators (Vāchaspati, *Bhāmatī*, ninth century; Ānandagiri, thirteenth century) link to Brahmadatta. He must therefore antedate the early eighth century. Terminus post quem: he engages a developed *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* problematic that presupposes the Mīmāṃsā-Vedānta interface mature enough to make the conjunction-thesis intelligible — pushing the lower bound to roughly the mid-sixth century. The window 550–700 CE follows Hiriyanna's (*Outlines of Indian Philosophy*, 1932) placement of pre-Śaṅkara Vedāntins of Brahmadatta's type and is broadly accepted by Thrasher (*The Advaita Vedānta of Brahma-Siddhi*, 1993) and Allen Thrasher's reconstruction of the *bhāvanā*-controversy. Note (AI-generated audit): the dating window depends entirely on (i) Sureśvara's standard chronology and (ii) the assumption that the polemical citations are not retrojective constructions of a generic opponent. Both are defensible but neither is independently corroborated. No Brahmadatta-text survives, so any sharper claim cannot be made.
core_thesis:
Brahmadatta's position is structurally important because it occupies the same dialectical space that Maṇḍana Miśra later takes (Maṇḍana, *Brahma-Siddhi*, *brahma-kāṇḍa* §§14–25 on *prasaṃkhyāna*) — and Maṇḍana, on Sureśvara's reading ([*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.67–68](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.67-68) and 4.41–44), is at most half-converted from it. The position has two coupled commitments. The first is *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*: knowledge of Brahman and prescribed Vedic action are conjointly the means of liberation, neither sufficient alone (the *pūrvapakṣa* reconstructed at [*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.51–55](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.51-55), attributed to Brahmadatta by Ānandagiri's *Tīkā* on the same passages). The second is *bhāvanā* or *prasaṃkhyāna*: knowledge of Brahman, even when correctly generated by *mahāvākya*-hearing, becomes liberating only through repeated meditative cultivation (Maṇḍana, *Brahma-Siddhi*, *brahma-kāṇḍa* §§14–25; the parallel position attributed to Brahmadatta at [*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.67](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.67)); a single act of upaniṣadic apprehension does not, by itself, terminate bondage. The two theses lock together. If knowledge alone — taken as a single cognitive event — were liberating, ritual action would be epistemically idle and *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* would collapse into *jñāna* alone. If knowledge requires *bhāvanā*-cultivation to become liberating, then sustained practice (which on the Mīmāṃsā analysis includes prescribed action) is constitutive of the path, and the conjunction-thesis is recovered (so Thrasher, *The Advaita Vedānta of Brahma-Siddhi*, 1993, ch. 3, on the dialectical interlock).

The philosophical force of Brahmadatta's position is that it takes Mīmāṃsā seriously as a continuous source of normative authority within Vedānta. The Mīmāṃsā analysis of Vedic injunction (*vidhi*) holds that scriptural sentences generate their effects through *bhāvanā* — the productive activity by which an enjoined end is brought about, structured by *sādhya* (what is to be effected), *sādhana* (the means), and *itikartavyatā* (the procedural specification) (Kumārila, *Tantra-Vārttika* on Jaimini, [*Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra* 2.1.1](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/2.1.1), the *bhāvanā*-vibhāga). Brahmadatta extends this analysis to *vedānta-vākya*: the *mahāvākya* 'tat tvam asi' (*Chāndogya-Upaniṣad* 6.8.7) is not, on his reading, a constative sentence whose mere apprehension immediately terminates ignorance. It is a teaching whose epistemic force unfolds through *bhāvanā* — repeated reflective and meditative cultivation that is the cognitive analogue of ritual repetition (the position Maṇḍana defends explicitly at *Brahma-Siddhi*, *brahma-kāṇḍa* §22). Knowledge so understood is not anti-ritual; it is the ritual-structure of cognition itself. *Karma* is not subordinated to or eliminated by *jñāna*; the two are aspects of one enjoined activity directed at the supreme end.

Sureśvara's destructive argument against Brahmadatta in the *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* (especially 1.67–69 and 4.41–44, the *prasaṃkhyāna-khaṇḍana*) is precisely that the *bhāvanā*-thesis cannot apply to *brahma-jñāna*. The argument is structural: Brahman is not an object to be effected (*sādhya*); it is the always-already accomplished, the *siddha-vastu* ([*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.67](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.67), contrasting *siddha-rūpa* and *sādhya-rūpa*). *Bhāvanā* is the cognitive mode appropriate to ends that are *to be brought about*; applied to what is already accomplished, it is category-mistaken ([*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 4.41–44](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/4.41-44)). Hence knowledge of Brahman is not generated by *bhāvanā* but by direct upaniṣadic cognition — *anubhava* — for which the *mahāvākya* is the immediate cause ([*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 2.81–95](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.81-95), on *mahāvākya-janya-anubhava*). Once the *bhāvanā*-thesis falls, *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* falls with it: action cannot supplement an immediate cognition that already terminates ignorance ([*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.69](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.69) with Sureśvara's framing).

What Brahmadatta would say in reply is preserved only obliquely. The natural reply is that Sureśvara's *siddha-vastu* / *sādhya* dichotomy begs the question, since on Brahmadatta's view what stands as 'always already accomplished' includes the *jīva*'s ignorance and ritual obligations as well as Brahman, and the cognitive recovery of identity is precisely a *bhāvanā*-process operating on the side of the *jīva*'s recognition rather than on the side of Brahman's metaphysical status (this is the structure of Maṇḍana's reply at *Brahma-Siddhi*, *brahma-kāṇḍa* §22). Maṇḍana's *Brahma-Siddhi* essentially makes this reply, defending *prasaṃkhyāna* as a continuing requirement even after the *mahāvākya* is heard (*Brahma-Siddhi*, *brahma-kāṇḍa* §§14–25). That Sureśvara wrote at length against both Brahmadatta and Maṇḍana — and that Vāchaspati's later *Bhāmatī*-school (*Bhāmatī* on *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.1.4) recovers a moderate *prasaṃkhyāna*-position — is the best evidence that Brahmadatta's argument was not easily dismissed (so Thrasher 1993, ch. 3). The position is not a strawman. It is the philosophically serious alternative to the Śaṅkara-Sureśvara immediacy-thesis, and the Advaita tradition's victory is the victory of one Vedāntic line over a coherent rival, not the elimination of an obvious confusion (Hiriyanna, *Outlines*, 1932; Thrasher 1993).

engaged_works.title: Lost commentary on Upaniṣads
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Work itself is lost; bhāvanā-thesis preserved through citations in Sureśvara's Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi and Vāchaspati's writings.
engaged_works.summary: Brahmadatta's commentary survives only through the polemical citations directed against it. Sureśvara's *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* (especially 1.67–69 on *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* and 4.41–44 on *prasaṃkhyāna*) names Brahmadatta as the proponent of two coupled theses: (i) *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*, that knowledge of Brahman and prescribed Vedic action together constitute the means of *mokṣa*, neither sufficient alone ([*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.51–55, 1.67](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.51-55, 1.67)); and (ii) *bhāvanā* or *prasaṃkhyāna*, that direct upaniṣadic cognition requires repeated meditative cultivation to become liberating ([*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 4.41–44](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/4.41-44); cf. Maṇḍana, *Brahma-Siddhi*, *brahma-kāṇḍa* §§14–25). The two are interlocking. If knowledge were liberating as a single event, *karma* would be idle; if knowledge requires *bhāvanā*-cultivation, then continuing practice is constitutive of the path, and the Mīmāṃsā framework of enjoined activity ([Jaimini, *Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra* 1.1.2](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2) with Śabara's *bhāṣya*; Kumārila, *Tantra-Vārttika* on [*Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra* 2.1.1](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/2.1.1)) extends to Vedānta. Maṇḍana Miśra's *Brahma-Siddhi* (*brahma-kāṇḍa* §§14–25) preserves the closely related *prasaṃkhyāna*-position that later commentators (Vāchaspati's *Bhāmatī* on *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.1.4; Ānandagiri's *Tīkā* on [*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.51](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.51)) explicitly link to Brahmadatta; whether Maṇḍana is independently developing the position or knowingly inheriting from Brahmadatta is disputed (Thrasher, *The Advaita Vedānta of Brahma-Siddhi*, 1993, ch. 3). Sureśvara's destructive argument is structural: Brahman is *siddha-vastu*, not *sādhya* ([*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.67](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.67)); *bhāvanā* applies to ends to be brought about, not to what is always-already accomplished ([*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 4.41](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/4.41)); therefore knowledge of Brahman is generated by direct upaniṣadic apprehension and not by meditative cultivation ([*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 2.81–95](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.81-95)). Brahmadatta's natural reply — that *bhāvanā* operates on the side of the *jīva*'s cognitive recovery, not on the side of Brahman's metaphysical status — is preserved only through Maṇḍana's defence of the position (*Brahma-Siddhi*, *brahma-kāṇḍa* §22). Nothing of Brahmadatta's actual commentary on the Upaniṣads survives; even the form of the work — *bhāṣya*, *vārttika*, or *prakaraṇa* — is uncertain (Hiriyanna, *Outlines*, 1932). The historical Brahmadatta is recoverable only as the named opponent across whom Śaṅkara's school defines its own immediacy-of-knowledge thesis; that the polemic was sustained over generations (Sureśvara, Vāchaspati, Ānandagiri) is itself evidence that the position was philosophically serious.

quoted_by.what_quoted: Brahmadatta named as proponent of *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* and *prasaṃkhyāna* / *bhāvanā* on cognition of Brahman; refuted at length.
Maṇḍana's *prasaṃkhyāna*-position parallels and partly inherits the Brahmadatta line (Ānandagiri's *Tīkā* on *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.51 makes the link explicit).
